headline: Islamic-State Debate Returns as PAS Anchors PN State Governments in Northern Malaysia
context: PAS directly governs Kelantan, Terengganu and Kedah, and anchors the PN coalition holding Perlis. Kelantan moved to re-enact provisions the Federal Court voided in Nik Elin (2024); Kedah passed Syariah Court institutional amendments in 2024.

cards.big: PAS controls Kelantan, Terengganu and Kedah and anchors PN's Perlis state government. The Islamic-state debate is back on the agenda.
cards.sub: Kelantan moved to re-enact provisions voided by the Federal Court in Nik Elin (2024); Kedah passed Syariah Court amendments. The Federal Court polices the line.

cards.big: The Federal Constitution limits Shariah courts to matters in the State List, primarily personal and family law.
cards.sub: Nik Elin (2024) voided Kelantan provisions touching federal criminal jurisdiction. The State List boundary is the controlling test.

cards.big: Federal Court rulings in recent years have repeatedly affirmed that civil law prevails where state Shariah enactments overreach the State List.
cards.sub: Cases challenging state enactments that touch federal subjects have generally been struck down on constitutional grounds.

cards.big: Malay-Muslim attitudes on Islamic governance are not monolithic. Pew (2013) found 86% favoured sharia as official law; Merdeka polls show narrower hudud support.
cards.sub: Support for sharia in principle is broad; support for hudud criminal-law implementation is narrower and varies with question framing.

cards.big: The debate is framed as secular-versus-Islamic. Survey data shows broader sharia-in-principle support but narrower hudud support — a middle ground that resists either binary label.
Like asking a country to choose between two rooms in a house that has 15. The binary framing erases the space where most positions actually sit.
cards.sub: The constitutional middle ground accommodates Shariah within the State List; the political question is whether any party builds a programme around it.

cards.big: The constitution already accommodates Islamic law within limits. The real debate is the scope of dual jurisdiction, and who decides where the line falls.
